reform_in_other_systems.description: Japan has the highest hospital bed density among wealthy nations — approximately 13 beds per 1,000 population versus 2.8 in the US. The country operates approximately 8,200 hospitals, the vast majority (70%) privately owned by physicians or medical corporations. Hospital consolidation into large systems has been limited by cultural norms (physician-owned hospitals as medical practices rather than investment vehicles), regulatory barriers to non-physician ownership, and the nationally regulated fee schedule that eliminates the pricing power that makes consolidation profitable in the US. When every hospital receives the same government-set price for a hip replacement, there is no revenue incentive to acquire the hospital across town.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrates the end-state that the US system structurally prevents: a competitive, fragmented hospital market with high bed availability, short wait times, and low costs. Japan's per capita healthcare spending ($4,700) is approximately one-third of the US level while delivering the highest life expectancy in the world and the lowest infant mortality rates. The structural lesson: hospital consolidation in the US is driven by the revenue opportunity created by unregulated pricing. Remove the pricing mechanism, and the consolidation incentive largely disappears. Japan's high bed density also means that hospital closures — which devastate US rural communities — are rare, because the system does not produce the financial distress that leads to closures.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Netherlands — Hospital Market Competition with Regulated Boundaries
reform_in_other_systems.description: The Netherlands introduced managed competition in hospital care in 2006 (Zorgverzekeringswet — Health Insurance Act), creating a system where private insurers negotiate prices with hospitals within a regulated framework. The Dutch Competition Authority (ACM) actively reviews hospital mergers and has blocked several transactions where consolidation would reduce competition below acceptable thresholds. Crucially, the Netherlands combines market competition with universal coverage and risk adjustment — insurers cannot deny coverage or charge risk-based premiums, removing the incentive for insurers to avoid sick patients. The system includes a 'macro budget control' mechanism: if national hospital spending exceeds the predetermined budget, overspending is clawed back through across-the-board rate reductions.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The Dutch model demonstrates that market competition in hospital care can work if: (1) antitrust enforcement is active and empowered (the ACM has blocked mergers the US FTC would likely have permitted); (2) universal coverage eliminates cost-shifting to uninsured patients; (3) risk adjustment prevents cream-skimming; and (4) macro budget controls prevent total spending from exceeding targets. Hospital prices in the Netherlands are lower than in the US despite the presence of private insurers and competitive hospital markets. The system shows that 'competition' is not the problem with the US healthcare market — the problem is unregulated competition that permits consolidation into monopolies.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Scotland — National Hospital Network with Regional Planning
reform_in_other_systems.description: Scotland's NHS operates 14 territorial health boards that plan and deliver hospital services regionally. Hospitals are publicly owned and operated as a national network rather than independent competing entities. Hospital placement, capacity, and specialty distribution are planned based on population health needs, geographic access, and clinical volume requirements — not market dynamics. There is no hospital pricing because there is no hospital market: hospitals are funded through global budgets allocated by health boards based on weighted capitation formulas (population adjusted for age, deprivation, and rurality). When a hospital in the Scottish Highlands needs investment, the decision is made based on population need, not profitability.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrates the alternative endpoint: a system where hospital planning replaces hospital competition entirely, eliminating both the consolidation problem and its consequences. Scotland's healthcare spending is approximately £2,800 per capita (~$3,500). Rural communities have guaranteed access to hospital services because the planning process ensures geographic coverage regardless of financial viability — the opposite of the US model where rural hospital closures (150+ since 2010) are driven by the financial logic that consolidation-oriented markets produce. The Scottish model's limitation is that it removes market incentives for efficiency and innovation — but it also removes market incentives for extraction, consolidation, and monopoly pricing.

issues.name: Rural healthcare collapse
issues.description: Over 150 rural hospitals have closed since 2010, and hundreds more are at risk. Rural Americans face longer travel times to care, fewer specialists, and worse outcomes. The closure pattern tracks with poverty and demographics — the communities that need healthcare most are losing it.

reformers.name: Alan Morgan and the National Rural Health Association
reformers.description: CEO of NRHA, the principal advocacy organization for rural health. Has testified before Congress over 30 times on rural hospital closures, workforce shortages, and the structural underfunding that drives rural healthcare collapse. NRHA represents over 21,000 members across rural health facilities and advocates for the Rural Emergency Hospital designation, telehealth expansion, and graduate medical education funding in rural areas.

reformers.name: Brock Slabach and the Cecil G. Sheps Center for Health Services Research
reformers.description: The Sheps Center at UNC maintains the most comprehensive database of rural hospital closures in the US — tracking every closure since 2005 with demographic, financial, and outcome data. Their data shows over 150 rural hospital closures since 2010, with the rate accelerating. The database has been the empirical foundation for virtually every federal and state rural health policy action.

reformers.name: Senators Jon Tester and Jerry Moran (Rural Health Caucus)
reformers.description: Bipartisan co-chairs of the Senate Rural Health Caucus. Tester (D-MT) and Moran (R-KS) have jointly sponsored legislation including the Rural Emergency Acute Care Hospital Act, the Rural Health Clinic Modernization Act, and provisions expanding telehealth in rural areas. The caucus represents one of the few genuinely bipartisan coalitions in Congress — rural health cuts across party lines because rural communities exist in both red and blue states.

reformers.name: Chartis Center for Rural Health (formerly iVantage Health Analytics)
reformers.description: Produces the annual Rural Hospital Vulnerability Index, scoring every rural hospital in the US on financial risk. Their 2023 analysis found that approximately 418 rural hospitals (over 30% of all rural hospitals) were vulnerable to closure based on financial indicators. The index has been adopted by CMS and state health departments as an early-warning system for targeted intervention.

reformers.name: Federal Office of Rural Health Policy (HRSA)
reformers.description: Federal coordinating office for rural health within the Health Resources and Services Administration. Administers over $300 million annually in rural health grants, supports State Offices of Rural Health, and operates the National Health Service Corps which places providers in underserved rural areas. Has expanded telehealth waivers and community health center funding, though critics note funding levels remain inadequate relative to the scale of closures.

legislation.name: Rural Emergency Hospital (REH) Designation
legislation.description: Created by the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021, allows rural hospitals that cannot sustain full inpatient operations to convert to emergency-only facilities while receiving enhanced Medicare payment (5% above standard outpatient rates) plus a monthly facility payment (~$272,000/month). Designed to prevent full closure by allowing hospitals to shed unsustainable inpatient beds while preserving emergency access. As of 2024, approximately 30 hospitals have converted to REH status — a slow uptake that reflects concerns about loss of inpatient revenue and community identity.

legislation.name: Medicare-Dependent Hospital and Low-Volume Hospital Payment Adjustments
legislation.description: Provides enhanced Medicare reimbursement for small rural hospitals where Medicare patients constitute a disproportionate share of volume. The adjustments have been repeatedly authorized on a temporary basis (typically 2-5 year extensions) rather than made permanent, creating chronic uncertainty that makes long-term planning and capital investment impossible for rural facilities. Each expiration cycle triggers lobbying, anxiety, and sometimes service reductions as hospitals prepare for potential payment cuts.

legislation.name: Medicaid Non-Expansion in Rural States
legislation.description: Ten states — disproportionately rural and Southern — have not expanded Medicaid, leaving rural hospitals with higher uncompensated care burdens and lower reimbursement. Studies show rural hospital closures are significantly more likely in non-expansion states: 75% of rural hospital closures since 2010 have occurred in states that did not expand Medicaid. The non-expansion decision creates a fiscal death spiral — uninsured patients generate uncompensated care that drives hospitals into insolvency.

legislation.name: Medicare Sequestration Cuts
legislation.description: Automatic 2% across-the-board cuts to Medicare reimbursement imposed under the Budget Control Act of 2011. For rural hospitals operating on margins of 1-3%, a 2% reduction in their largest revenue source (Medicare often represents 50-70% of rural hospital revenue) is the difference between solvency and insolvency. Sequestration was temporarily suspended during COVID but has been partially reimposed.

legislation.name: Telehealth Expansion (COVID-era waivers and proposed permanent extensions)
legislation.description: During COVID, CMS waived restrictions on telehealth: allowing services across state lines, permitting audio-only visits, expanding eligible provider types, and reimbursing telehealth at parity with in-person visits. These waivers dramatically expanded access in rural areas where specialist travel distances average 40+ miles. Congressional proposals to make waivers permanent (CONNECT for Health Act, Telehealth Modernization Act) have advanced but not fully passed, creating uncertainty about whether rural telehealth access will be sustained.

structurally_incentivized.name: Large Health Systems Pursuing Urban-Suburban Growth
structurally_incentivized.description: Major health systems allocate capital to urban and suburban markets where commercial insurance reimbursement is highest and population density supports volume. Rural hospitals — with older, sicker, more publicly insured populations and lower volumes — offer inferior returns on investment.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Capital allocation follows margin. A suburban ambulatory surgery center generates 20-30% margins on commercially insured orthopedic cases; a rural critical access hospital generates 1-3% margins on a Medicare-dominant payer mix. When health systems acquire rural hospitals, the rural facility competes for capital against higher-return urban investments within the same system — and loses. Acquired rural hospitals often see service reductions and eventual closure as the system redirects resources to more profitable markets.

structurally_incentivized.name: Physician Workforce Pipeline (Academic Medical Centers)
structurally_incentivized.description: Medical schools and residency programs are overwhelmingly urban-based. Only 5.5% of physicians practice in rural areas despite 20% of the population living there. The pipeline is structurally oriented toward urban practice through training location, specialty selection (rural areas need primary care; training incentivizes specialization), and spouse/partner career considerations.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Medical education debt averaging $200,000+ incentivizes high-paying specialty practice in urban markets. Residency programs in urban academic centers socialize trainees into urban practice patterns. The National Health Service Corps and loan forgiveness programs reach a fraction of the need. The pipeline will not self-correct because the incentive structure — debt, specialty premium, training location, lifestyle — all point toward urban practice.

structurally_incentivized.name: Private Insurers Exiting Rural Markets
structurally_incentivized.description: Commercial insurers have steadily reduced participation in rural individual and small-group markets where the risk pool is small, older, and sicker. In many rural counties, only one insurer participates in the ACA marketplace. Limited insurer competition means lower negotiated rates for rural hospitals, further compressing margins.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Insurers maximize profit by covering large, healthy, employed populations. Rural markets offer the opposite: small, older, sicker populations with higher utilization. Exiting rural markets (or offering only high-deductible plans with narrow networks that exclude the local hospital) is economically rational for insurers but devastating for rural healthcare infrastructure.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Scotland — Geographic Guarantee of Hospital Access
reform_in_other_systems.description: Scotland's NHS operates regional health boards that guarantee hospital access based on population need and geography, not financial viability. Rural hospitals in the Scottish Highlands receive funding through weighted capitation formulas that adjust for remoteness, age, and deprivation — ensuring that low-volume facilities receive sufficient resources regardless of market conditions.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Rural communities in Scotland have guaranteed access to emergency and essential hospital services. No Scottish rural hospital has been 'closed' in the American sense — restructured services yes, but geographic access to emergency care is maintained as a public commitment. Scotland spends approximately £2,800 per capita on healthcare versus $12,500 in the US.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Norway — Decentralized Hospital Network with National Standards
reform_in_other_systems.description: Norway maintains a network of small local hospitals across its sparsely populated geography (5.4 million people spread over terrain more rugged than Appalachia), funded through national hospital trusts. Distance penalties in the funding formula provide additional resources for remote facilities.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Norway's rural population has access to emergency and essential hospital services within 90 minutes for over 99% of the population, despite extreme geographic challenges. The system costs approximately $7,000 per capita — about 55% of the US rate — while delivering universal access and life expectancy 4 years longer than the US.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Australia — Rural Health Workforce Incentives (Bonded Medical Places, MDRAP)
reform_in_other_systems.description: Australia addresses rural health workforce shortages through structured incentive programs: bonded medical school places requiring rural service, the Modified Monash Model classifying remoteness for targeted incentives, the Medical Deputising and Recruitment for Areas of Priority program, and substantially higher Medicare rebates for rural practitioners (up to 50% premium over urban rates).
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: While Australia still has rural-urban health disparities, the incentive structure has increased rural medical workforce supply significantly. Rural GPs earn 20-50% more than urban counterparts through the incentive structure, creating genuine financial motivation for rural practice. The approach demonstrates that pipeline problems require financial solutions — aspirational programs without financial incentives do not move practitioners to underserved areas.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Canada — Rural and Northern Health Strategy
reform_in_other_systems.description: Canada addresses rural healthcare through a combination of: enhanced fee-for-service payments in rural areas, the Northern and Rural Recruitment and Retention Initiative, fly-in medical teams for remote Indigenous communities, and provincial programs that forgive medical school debt in exchange for rural service commitments.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Canada's rural health outcomes, while still lagging urban, are significantly better than US rural outcomes. Life expectancy in rural Canada exceeds life expectancy in rural America by approximately 3-5 years depending on region. The system demonstrates that public funding of rural healthcare — rather than reliance on market viability — produces better access and outcomes.

issues.name: Maternal mortality crisis
issues.description: The United States has the highest maternal mortality rate of any wealthy nation — approximately 22.3 deaths per 100,000 live births (2022 CDC data), compared to 2.8 in Norway, 3.4 in the Netherlands, 4.6 in Germany, and 5.5 in the United Kingdom. The US rate has been rising for two decades while peer nations' rates decline. The disparity is even starker by race: Black women die at 2.6 times the rate of white women (49.5 vs. 19.1 per 100,000 in 2022); Indigenous/Alaska Native women at 2.0 times the rate. The Black maternal mortality rate alone (49.5 per 100,000) is comparable to rates in developing nations and represents the widest racial health disparity in American medicine. Approximately 80% of pregnancy-related deaths are preventable, according to CDC Maternal Mortality Review Committees (MMRCs) that investigate every pregnancy-related death — meaning the US system is not failing at the margins but at the core. The leading clinical causes — cardiovascular conditions (including cardiomyopathy and hemorrhage), mental health conditions (including suicide and overdose), and infection/sepsis — are all treatable when identified and addressed. The causes are systemic, not individual: fragmented insurance coverage that creates gaps during and after pregnancy (Medicaid, which covers 42% of US births, historically terminated coverage at 60 days postpartum — before the period of highest maternal death risk); hospital closures in underserved areas that have created 'maternity care deserts' in over 1,100 US counties (35% of all counties), affecting 2.3 million women of childbearing age who have no obstetric hospital, birth center, or OB-GYN/midwife within their county; provider bias that systematically dismisses women's — especially Black women's — reported symptoms (studies show physicians are less likely to take Black women's pain reports seriously and more likely to attribute complications to patient behavior rather than medical emergency); and a healthcare delivery model that invests heavily in high-tech intervention while systematically undervaluing the midwifery, doula, and community health worker models that demonstrably reduce mortality in every country that uses them. The US has the highest C-section rate (32%) and lowest midwifery utilization (12% of births) among wealthy nations — exactly the inverse of countries with the lowest maternal mortality. Severe maternal morbidity — life-threatening complications that stop short of death — affects an additional 60,000 women per year and has been increasing at an even faster rate than mortality, suggesting the full scope of maternal health system failure is significantly larger than the death count alone reveals.

reformers.name: Dr. Joia Crear-Perry and the National Birth Equity Collaborative
reformers.description: OB-GYN and the most influential voice reframing maternal mortality as a structural racism and health equity crisis rather than an individual risk factor problem. Founded the National Birth Equity Collaborative in 2015 to address the root causes of racial disparities in birth outcomes through policy change, provider training, and community-based care models. Has testified before Congress, advised CMS, and served on the National Quality Forum Perinatal and Reproductive Health Committee. Her core argument, grounded in decades of epidemiological data, demolishes the 'individual risk factor' explanation: college-educated Black women have worse maternal outcomes than white women without a high school diploma. Black women with private insurance have higher maternal mortality than white women on Medicaid. The variable is not income, education, insurance status, or health behavior — the variable is structural racism operating through: residential segregation that determines hospital quality; provider bias that dismisses reported symptoms; chronic stress from racial discrimination (weathering hypothesis); and a healthcare system designed around white middle-class norms. Crear-Perry has been instrumental in shifting the policy discourse from 'how do we fix Black women' to 'how do we fix the system that harms Black women.'

reformers.name: Black Mamas Matter Alliance
reformers.description: National cross-sector network of over 30 Black women-led organizations centering Black maternal health, rights, and justice. Founded in 2016. Leads the annual Black Maternal Health Week (April 11-17, recognized by Congressional resolution and over 30 state governors). BMMA's approach is rooted in reproductive justice — the framework developed by Black women organizers (SisterSong) that connects reproductive health to the broader conditions needed to raise families: housing, income, safety, environmental quality, and freedom from discrimination. BMMA advocates for: Medicaid postpartum extension (achieved — 46 states adopted); Momnibus Act passage; diversifying the perinatal workforce (less than 6% of OB-GYNs and 2% of midwives are Black); community-based doula programs; and centering the leadership and voices of Black birthing people in designing the systems meant to serve them. BMMA's Black Maternal Health Momnibus Toolkit has been adopted by legislators in 22 states as a framework for state-level policy action.

reformers.name: Charles Johnson IV and 4Kira4Moms
reformers.description: Charles Johnson's wife, Kira Dixon Johnson, died from internal bleeding following a routine cesarean section at Cedars-Sinai Medical Center in Los Angeles in 2016. Despite Kira being a healthy 39-year-old marathon runner with no risk factors, staff failed to act on multiple clinical warning signs of hemorrhage over a 10-hour period — during which her blood-pressure dropped, her hemoglobin crashed, and her abdomen distended visibly — while the family pleaded for intervention. An internal review found that Kira's calls for help were dismissed or deferred. Johnson founded 4Kira4Moms and has become one of the most visible advocates for maternal safety, testifying before Congress and meeting with three successive White House administrations. His advocacy was instrumental in the passage of the Momnibus Act provisions and the Preventing Maternal Deaths Act reauthorization. Johnson's case illustrates the intersection of racial bias and systemic failure: Kira was a wealthy, educated, insured Black woman at one of the nation's most prestigious hospitals — and she died from a treatable condition because her symptoms were dismissed.

reformers.name: March of Dimes
reformers.description: Publishes the annual Maternity Care Desert report — the most comprehensive mapping of US counties without adequate obstetric care. The 2023 report documented that 35% of US counties (over 1,100 counties) are full maternity care deserts: no hospital with obstetric services, no birth center, no OB-GYN or certified nurse-midwife. An additional 5% of counties have limited maternity care access. Over 2.3 million women of childbearing age live in these deserts. The geographic distribution tracks with poverty, race, and rurality: Southern and Appalachian states are disproportionately affected. Between 2020 and 2022, an additional 217 hospitals stopped providing obstetric services — accelerating the desert formation. March of Dimes' data has driven federal action including the Rural Emergency Hospital designation (which provides enhanced Medicare payment for critical access hospitals) and state-level investments in telemedicine for prenatal care. The organization also publishes a Premature Birth Report Card grading states on preterm birth rates, identifying geographic and racial disparities in outcomes.

reformers.name: Dr. Elizabeth Howell
reformers.description: Perelman School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania. Her research program has produced the most rigorous quantification of the 'hospital quality gap' in maternal care: the finding that the hospitals where Black women deliver are, on average, of lower quality than the hospitals where white women deliver — and that this difference in delivery site accounts for a significant portion of the racial mortality disparity. Her landmark 2016 study in the American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology found that Black women were more likely to deliver at hospitals with higher risk-adjusted severe maternal morbidity rates for all patients (including white patients), suggesting that the disparity is partly a segregation-of-care problem — structural racism determines where you give birth, and where you give birth determines your survival odds. Her subsequent work estimated that if Black women delivered at the same hospitals as white women, the Black severe maternal morbidity rate could be reduced by approximately 47%. This finding has profound policy implications: it shifts the intervention target from individual provider bias (which is real but hard to change) to hospital quality and access (which is addressable through investment, regulation, and desegregation of care).

reformers.name: Every Mother Counts
reformers.description: Founded in 2010 by Christy Turlington Burns after her own near-fatal postpartum hemorrhage following the birth of her daughter. The organization has invested over $25 million in maternal health programs across the US and in 10+ countries, funding community-based initiatives including: doula programs in underserved communities, birth center development, midwifery education scholarships, and community health worker training. Every Mother Counts focuses on closing the evidence-to-practice gap: the evidence overwhelmingly supports midwifery-led care, doula support, and postpartum home visits as effective interventions that reduce mortality, but the US healthcare system systematically underfunds these evidence-based approaches in favor of higher-cost, higher-tech interventions that are less effective. The organization's documentary work (films 'No Woman, No Cry' and 'Giving Birth in America') has raised public awareness of both the US maternal mortality crisis and global maternal health challenges, reaching over 50 million viewers.

reformers.name: Dr. Rachel Hardeman
reformers.description: University of Minnesota School of Public Health reproductive health equity researcher. Her work has advanced the field's understanding of how structural racism — not individual racial identity — drives maternal health disparities. Key contributions: demonstrating that structural racism measures at the community level (residential segregation, incarceration rates, educational inequity) are better predictors of adverse maternal outcomes than individual-level factors; showing that Black newborns are three times more likely to die in hospital when cared for by white physicians versus Black physicians (Greenwood et al., 2020, PNAS — a study Hardeman co-authored that demonstrated a concordance effect with survival implications); and developing the Structural Racism Effect Index (SREI) for use in health research. Her work has shifted federal research priorities: NIH now explicitly funds research on structural racism and health outcomes, in part due to the methodological frameworks Hardeman's team developed.

reformers.name: National Partnership for Women & Families
reformers.description: Policy advocacy organization that has driven federal and state maternal health legislation for over 25 years. Instrumental in: the passage of the Preventing Maternal Deaths Act (2018 and 2022 reauthorization); the Medicaid postpartum extension (advocated for the 12-month extension included in the American Rescue Plan); and the development of CMS maternal health quality measures. The organization's Maternity Care Quality Campaign coordinates over 40 national organizations working on maternal health policy. Their reports on maternal health disparities have been cited in Congressional hearings, White House briefings, and state legislative debates. The National Partnership brings the policy infrastructure — legislative drafting, coalition coordination, regulatory comment, and implementation monitoring — that translates advocacy into law.

legislation.name: Postpartum Medicaid Extension (American Rescue Plan Act provision)
legislation.description: The American Rescue Plan (2021) gave states the option to extend Medicaid postpartum coverage from 60 days to 12 months through a State Plan Amendment (SPA). This addressed one of the most glaring coverage gaps in the US healthcare system: Medicaid covers 42% of US births, but historically terminated coverage just 60 days after delivery — a cutoff that preceded the period of highest maternal death risk. CDC Maternal Mortality Review Committee data shows that approximately 53% of pregnancy-related deaths occur between 7 days and 1 year postpartum. The 60-day cutoff meant that the majority of maternal deaths occurred during a coverage gap. As of January 2024, 46 states and the District of Columbia have adopted the 12-month extension (with four remaining states expected to adopt). CMS estimates the extension provides continuous coverage for over 720,000 additional postpartum individuals annually. The Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2023 made the extension option permanent (the ARP provision was temporary). Evidence from early-adopting states shows increased postpartum visit attendance, increased depression screening, and increased contraception access — all factors associated with reduced maternal mortality.

legislation.name: Preventing Maternal Deaths Act
legislation.description: Authorized CDC funding for state Maternal Mortality Review Committees (MMRCs) — multidisciplinary panels that investigate every pregnancy-related death in their jurisdiction to determine cause, timing, preventability, and contributing factors. The original 2018 legislation provided $12 million annually for five years; the 2022 reauthorization (Preventing Maternal Deaths Reauthorization Act) expanded funding to $60 million annually through 2027 and broadened scope to include severe maternal morbidity (near-miss cases). By 2023, all 50 states, Washington DC, and three territories had established MMRCs — creating the first comprehensive national surveillance system for maternal death. The MMRCs' most significant finding, replicated across states: approximately 80% of pregnancy-related deaths are preventable. Contributing factors identified include: lack of continuity of care (51%), patient/family lack of knowledge of warning signs (46%), clinical misdiagnosis (33%), and failure to refer to appropriate provider (29%). These findings demonstrate that the crisis is driven by system failures — not by the inherent danger of pregnancy, which is lower than at any point in human history from a purely medical standpoint. The MMRCs have also been critical in quantifying racial disparities with clinical specificity: the committees have documented that Black women's symptoms are more likely to be dismissed, their care transitions are more likely to be fragmented, and their postpartum follow-up is less likely to occur.

legislation.name: Black Maternal Health Momnibus Act (introduced, partially enacted)
legislation.description: Comprehensive legislative package introduced by Rep. Lauren Underwood (IL-14, a nurse and the youngest Black woman elected to Congress) and Sen. Cory Booker (NJ) in 2021, consisting of 13 individual bills addressing maternal health through multiple pathways: investment in social determinants of health (housing, nutrition, transportation); diversifying the perinatal workforce (funding for midwifery education, doula training, and community health worker programs, particularly for people of color); improving data collection (disaggregated data by race, ethnicity, and geography); expanding digital tools (telehealth for prenatal and postpartum care in rural areas); addressing environmental impacts on maternal health (lead exposure, air quality, climate-related heat stress); supporting maternal mental health (screening, referral, and treatment for perinatal mood disorders); expanding community-based models of care; and supporting maternal health innovation. Several provisions have been enacted individually: the Protecting Moms Who Served Act (VA maternal health improvements), portions of the MAMA's Act (Medicaid data collection), and elements incorporated into appropriations bills. The full package has not passed either chamber. The Momnibus represents the most comprehensive federal legislative response to the maternal mortality crisis — its scope reflects the understanding that maternal mortality is driven by multiple intersecting systems, not a single clinical cause.

legislation.name: California Maternal Quality Care Collaborative (CMQCC)
legislation.description: Not federal legislation but a state-level quality improvement initiative that has become the national and international model for reducing maternal mortality through standardized clinical protocols. CMQCC, established at Stanford University in 2006, developed and implemented evidence-based maternal safety bundles — standardized sets of clinical protocols for the leading causes of maternal death: obstetric hemorrhage (2010), preeclampsia/eclampsia (2014), venous thromboembolism (2015), and cardiovascular conditions in pregnancy (2017). Each bundle includes: readiness (supplies, equipment, protocols in place before an emergency); recognition (standardized assessment tools to identify deterioration early); response (step-by-step intervention protocols); and reporting (data collection for quality improvement). The hemorrhage bundle alone includes quantitative blood loss measurement (replacing the notoriously inaccurate 'eyeball' estimation that was standard practice), hemorrhage carts pre-stocked on all labor and delivery units, massive transfusion protocols, and early escalation criteria. CMQCC worked with every birthing hospital in California (over 250 hospitals) to implement these bundles through collaborative quality improvement rather than regulation. California's maternal mortality rate dropped by 55% between 2006 and 2016 (from approximately 17 to 7 per 100,000) while the national rate rose by approximately 27% over the same period. The model has been adopted nationally through the Alliance for Innovation on Maternal Health (AIM), which has enrolled over 1,800 hospitals in 46 states.

legislation.name: Medicaid Coverage of Doula Services
legislation.description: As of 2024, 14 states have approved or implemented Medicaid reimbursement for doula services (community-based, non-clinical support during pregnancy, birth, and postpartum). Oregon, Minnesota, and Florida were among the first states to provide coverage. The evidence supporting doula care is robust: a 2017 Cochrane Review (the gold standard of medical evidence synthesis) found that continuous labor support (including doulas) is associated with: 25% reduction in cesarean delivery, 8% increase in spontaneous vaginal birth, 10% reduction in use of any pain medication, shorter labor duration, higher satisfaction, and higher 5-minute Apgar scores. A 2016 study in Birth found that Medicaid recipients receiving doula support in the Minneapolis-St. Paul area had 22% lower odds of cesarean delivery and 35% lower odds of preterm birth. The average cost of doula care is $800-$2,000 per birth; the average cost savings from a prevented cesarean section is $15,000-$25,000. Despite this evidence and the favorable cost-benefit ratio, Medicaid doula reimbursement rates in most states are inadequate ($350-$900 total for prenatal, birth, and postpartum support), creating a workforce sustainability challenge.

legislation.name: Medicaid Coverage Gap in Non-Expansion States
legislation.description: Ten states (as of 2024) have not expanded Medicaid under the ACA: Texas, Georgia, Mississippi, Alabama, Tennessee, South Carolina, Kansas, Wyoming, Wisconsin (partial expansion), and Florida (rejected ballot initiative implementation). This leaves an estimated 1.9 million adults in the coverage gap — earning too much for traditional Medicaid but too little for ACA marketplace subsidies. The gap disproportionately affects Black and Hispanic women in the Deep South. The correlation between Medicaid non-expansion and maternal mortality is not coincidental — it is causal: a 2020 study in the American Journal of Preventive Medicine (Eliason, 2020) found that Medicaid expansion was associated with a 7.01 reduction in maternal mortality per 100,000 births. The non-expansion states contain 7 of the 10 states with the highest maternal mortality rates. In non-expansion states, a pregnant woman may qualify for pregnancy-specific Medicaid but lose coverage at 60 days postpartum (or 12 months with the new extension) and have no coverage pathway until she becomes pregnant again — creating a coverage cycle that prevents continuous primary care, chronic disease management, and family planning. Texas, the largest non-expansion state, has the highest number of uninsured women of childbearing age (over 1.2 million) and a maternal mortality rate approximately 50% above the national average.

legislation.name: Certificate of Need Laws Limiting Birth Center Expansion
legislation.description: Thirty-five states and the District of Columbia maintain Certificate of Need (CON) laws that require state regulatory approval before opening new healthcare facilities, including freestanding birth centers. The CON process was originally designed (1974 National Health Planning and Resources Development Act) to prevent overbuilding and unnecessary duplication of services. In practice, CON laws function as incumbent protection: existing hospital systems use the CON process to block competitors from entering their markets. Hospitals file objections to new birth center applications, funding legal challenges that can take 2-4 years to resolve and cost applicants $100,000-$500,000 in legal fees — effectively barring entry by community-based organizations and independent midwifery practices. Freestanding birth centers provide midwife-led care at approximately 40-60% of hospital birth costs ($2,000-$6,000 vs. $10,000-$30,000+) with equivalent or better outcomes for low-risk pregnancies: birth center births are associated with C-section rates of 6-10% (vs. 32% for hospital births) and significantly lower rates of medical intervention. The Strong Start for Mothers and Newborns evaluation (CMS, 2018) found that Medicaid enrollees receiving prenatal care at birth centers had 44% lower preterm birth rates and total costs 21% lower than matched comparison women receiving hospital-based care. CON laws structurally protect the hospital monopoly on birth care while communities — especially rural and underserved communities where hospitals have closed obstetric units — lose access to the lower-cost, evidence-based alternative.

legislation.name: Restrictive Scope-of-Practice Laws for Midwives
legislation.description: Many states restrict Certified Professional Midwives (CPMs) and Certified Nurse-Midwives (CNMs) through: requirements for physician supervision or collaboration agreements (requiring a physician to formally agree to supervise the midwife's practice — in underserved areas where no physician is available, this requirement makes midwifery practice impossible); prohibition of autonomous midwifery practice; licensure barriers for CPMs (CPMs are not licensed in 15 states); restrictions or prohibition of out-of-hospital birth; and exclusion of midwives from hospital privileging. The United States uses midwives for approximately 12% of births — the lowest rate among wealthy nations. In the Netherlands, midwives attend approximately 50% of births. In the UK, midwife-led care is the default for low-risk pregnancies. In Scandinavia, midwives manage the vast majority of normal births. The evidence is unambiguous: countries with the highest midwifery utilization have the lowest maternal mortality rates. A 2019 Lancet series on midwifery estimated that 'educated, trained, regulated, and integrated' midwifery could avert 83% of maternal deaths globally. US scope-of-practice restrictions are driven by physician specialty organizations' lobbying (primarily the American Medical Association and state medical associations), not by evidence. Every major systematic review (Cochrane, Lancet, WHO) supports expanded midwifery-led care for low-risk pregnancies.

legislation.name: Hyde Amendment — Restriction on Federal Funding for Abortion
legislation.description: Annual appropriations rider (renewed since 1977) prohibiting federal Medicaid funding for abortion except in cases of rape, incest, or life endangerment. The Hyde Amendment forces low-income individuals who need abortion care to: carry pregnancies to term regardless of health risk; delay care for weeks while raising funds (an estimated $400-$1,500 for first-trimester abortion, more for later procedures); travel to states with coverage or to providers who offer reduced-cost care; or seek unsafe alternatives. Each of these pathways increases maternal health risk. Carrying an unwanted pregnancy to term is, by definition, forced continuation of a condition that carries inherent mortality risk — the maternal mortality rate for carrying to term (approximately 23 per 100,000) is approximately 14 times higher than for abortion (approximately 0.4 per 100,000 for legal first-trimester abortion). The forced delay of abortion into later gestational ages (while raising funds, arranging travel, navigating waiting periods) increases both medical risk and cost. The Hyde Amendment disproportionately affects Black and Indigenous women, who are more likely to rely on Medicaid and more likely to face the concentration of state-level abortion restrictions that compound the federal funding prohibition. The Dobbs decision (2022) and subsequent state abortion bans have intensified the Hyde Amendment's impact: in states where abortion is banned or severely restricted, the combination of Hyde (no federal funding) and state bans (no legal access) eliminates abortion care entirely for low-income individuals.

legislation.name: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022) and State Abortion Bans
legislation.description: The Supreme Court's June 2022 decision overturning Roe v. Wade eliminated the constitutional right to abortion and returned regulation to individual states. As of 2024, 14 states have enacted near-total abortion bans and an additional 7 have imposed gestational limits of 6-15 weeks. The bans are concentrated in states with the highest maternal mortality rates and the largest racial disparities: Mississippi (highest maternal mortality), Alabama, Louisiana, Tennessee, Georgia, and Texas. The maternal health consequences are direct and measurable: forced continuation of pregnancy increases maternal mortality risk by definition (pregnancy carries a mortality rate approximately 14x higher than legal abortion); delays in treating ectopic pregnancies and pregnancy complications due to provider uncertainty about legal liability have been documented in multiple states; and OB-GYN residency applications in ban states have declined 10-30%, threatening the physician pipeline in states already underserved. Texas reported a 56% increase in infant deaths from congenital anomalies in the year following its ban — conditions that would have been identified via prenatal screening and for which abortion was a medical option before the ban. The interaction between abortion bans and maternal mortality is not theoretical — it is now generating measurable excess deaths and complications in real time.
legislation.jurisdiction: US (14 states with near-total bans, 7 with severe restrictions)

structurally_incentivized.name: Hospital Systems (Revenue from High-Intervention, High-Throughput Birth)
structurally_incentivized.description: The US cesarean section rate is approximately 32% — well above the WHO-recommended 10-15% rate for optimal outcomes. The variation by hospital ranges from 7% to over 70% for low-risk patients, a variation that cannot be explained by clinical factors and tracks closely with hospital financial structure. A cesarean delivery generates approximately $15,000-$25,000 more in revenue than a vaginal delivery (average commercial insurance payment: $26,000-$35,000 for C-section vs. $14,000-$20,000 for vaginal). US hospitals perform approximately 1.2 million cesarean sections annually, generating approximately $30 billion in additional revenue compared to vaginal birth. Hospital revenue is maximized by interventionist birth practices: continuous electronic fetal monitoring (shown in multiple randomized trials to increase C-section rates by 50-66% without improving neonatal outcomes), labor induction (associated with 2-3x higher odds of C-section in nulliparous women), epidural anesthesia (associated with longer labor and higher intervention rates), and surgical delivery. The US births-per-bed model incentivizes faster throughput: induction and scheduled cesareans are more predictable and profitable than physiologic labor, which can last 24-48 hours and requires continuous nursing support.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Fee-for-service reimbursement pays more for procedures than for watchful waiting, more for surgery than for physiologic birth support, and more for technology than for human care. Hospitals earn more from a complicated birth than an uncomplicated one. A birth with an epidural, continuous monitoring, labor induction, and cesarean delivery generates 3-5x the revenue of a physiologic birth with midwifery support. The financial structure rewards intervention over patience, specialization over primary care, and technology over human support — exactly the inverse of what the evidence says reduces maternal mortality. Attempts to shift reimbursement toward bundled payment models (a single payment for the entire episode of maternity care, incentivizing lower-cost approaches) have been resisted by hospital associations that would see revenue decline under value-based payment. The system is optimized for revenue, not for maternal survival.

structurally_incentivized.name: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) and State Medical Associations — Physician Workforce Protection
structurally_incentivized.description: ACOG, the professional organization representing approximately 60,000 OB-GYNs, has historically opposed or constrained the expansion of midwifery scope of practice and autonomous midwife-led birth care. While ACOG's formal public positions have evolved — a 2017 joint statement with the American College of Nurse-Midwives acknowledged midwifery value — the organization's state-level advocacy through state medical associations frequently opposes: CPM licensure, autonomous CNM practice (without mandatory physician collaboration agreements), birth center development, and Medicaid coverage of midwifery-led care at parity with physician-led care. The financial dimension is significant: OB-GYNs earn a median of $312,000 annually (2023 Medscape survey); certified nurse-midwives earn approximately $120,000. Expanding midwifery-led care for the estimated 85% of pregnancies that are low-risk would redirect a significant portion of birth revenue from physicians to midwives — billions annually in a $50+ billion maternity care market.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Professional guild protection through regulatory capture at the state level. OB-GYNs and their state medical associations control the legislative and regulatory processes that determine who can provide birth care through: dominance on state medical and nursing boards; scope-of-practice lobbying (spending millions annually at the state level to maintain physician supervision requirements for midwives); collaboration agreement requirements that give individual physicians veto power over midwifery practice in their market; and hospital privileging decisions that exclude midwives from hospital-based birth care. The evidence overwhelmingly supports midwife-led care for low-risk births: lower C-section rates (12-15% vs. 32%), lower intervention rates, equivalent or better outcomes, lower cost ($5,000-$8,000 vs. $15,000-$30,000), and higher patient satisfaction (Cochrane Review, 2016). But expanding midwifery autonomy reduces OB-GYN market share. The result: the US has the lowest midwifery utilization and the highest maternal mortality of any wealthy nation. These two facts are related.

structurally_incentivized.name: Medical Malpractice Insurance Industry
structurally_incentivized.description: OB-GYN malpractice premiums are among the highest of any specialty — $100,000-$300,000+ annually depending on state (exceeding $300,000 in Florida, Illinois, and New York). Approximately 75-80% of OB-GYNs will be sued for malpractice during their career. The high-premium environment drives defensive medicine: ordering more tests, performing more interventions, and delivering by C-section in situations where watchful waiting would be clinically appropriate — because 'I did everything possible' is a stronger legal defense than 'I observed and waited.' Defensive C-sections — performed to manage legal risk rather than clinical risk — are estimated to account for 5-20% of the excess US cesarean rate. The malpractice environment also contributes to OB-GYN shortages in rural and underserved areas: malpractice premium costs are unaffordable for lower-volume rural practices, driving OB-GYNs away from the communities with the greatest need and contributing to maternity care desert formation.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Malpractice premiums incentivize interventionist practice because intervention is legally defensible and non-intervention is legally risky. The paradox is vicious and circular: defensive medicine increases interventions → increased interventions increase complication rates → complications generate malpractice claims → claims drive premium increases → higher premiums incentivize more defensive medicine. The cycle enriches the malpractice insurance industry (which profits from higher premiums regardless of claim outcomes) while degrading care quality. The malpractice system also structurally suppresses innovation: any departure from standard (interventionist) practice, even when evidence-based (e.g., midwife-led care, physiologic birth management, out-of-hospital birth), creates litigation exposure for the provider and the facility. The system punishes clinical courage and rewards clinical conformity — and the standard it enforces is the high-intervention model that produces the outcomes it then litigates.

structurally_incentivized.name: Private Insurance Industry (Coverage Design That Fragments Maternity Care and Excludes Evidence-Based Alternatives)
structurally_incentivized.description: Private insurers structure maternity benefits to minimize per-service cost while maximizing administrative revenue: narrow networks that exclude midwives and freestanding birth centers; separate maternity deductibles that can exceed $3,000-$5,000; exclusion of doula services (despite a 25% C-section reduction and favorable cost-benefit ratio); denial of coverage for home birth with a licensed midwife; limited or no coverage for lactation support (which reduces postpartum complications); and aggressive prior authorization and utilization review for maternity services. The coverage design systematically excludes the lower-cost, evidence-based interventions that reduce maternal mortality (midwifery care, doula support, birth center birth, postpartum home visits) while covering the higher-cost, higher-intervention approaches (hospital birth, surgical delivery, NICU stays resulting from unnecessary interventions). The average out-of-pocket cost for maternity care with employer-sponsored insurance is approximately $2,800 for vaginal delivery and $3,400 for cesarean — costs that deter prenatal care attendance and postpartum follow-up among lower-income insured families.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Insurers' short-term cost minimization through service exclusion creates long-term cost escalation through intervention cascades. Denying coverage for a $1,500 doula that reduces C-section risk by 25% results in paying for a $25,000 C-section. Excluding birth centers (average cost: $4,000-$6,000) and covering only hospital births ($15,000-$30,000) increases per-birth spending by 3-5x for low-risk pregnancies. Limiting postpartum visits to one 6-week check-up (the standard — versus the WHO recommendation of 3+ contacts in the first 6 weeks) misses the window for detecting postpartum hemorrhage, infection, hypertensive disorders, and depression — the leading causes of late maternal death. The misalignment is structural: insurers optimize for quarterly financial performance, not for longitudinal maternal health. The administrative overhead of maternity coverage — prior authorizations, network management, claim adjudication, utilization review — adds 15-25% to the cost of maternity care while providing no clinical value.

structurally_incentivized.name: Pharmaceutical and Medical Device Companies (Pitocin, Epidural Supplies, Fetal Monitoring Equipment)
structurally_incentivized.description: The pharmaceutical and medical device industries profit directly from the high-intervention US birth model. Pitocin (synthetic oxytocin, manufactured primarily by Par Pharmaceutical/Endo) is used in approximately 50% of US births for labor induction or augmentation — a rate far exceeding other wealthy nations. Epidural anesthesia supplies and services represent a significant revenue stream for anesthesia providers and hospitals. Electronic fetal monitoring equipment (GE Healthcare, Philips, Corometrics) is used in virtually 100% of US hospital births despite evidence that continuous monitoring increases C-section rates without improving outcomes (vs. intermittent auscultation, which is standard in countries with lower maternal mortality). Each of these interventions generates revenue for manufacturers and providers. The installed base of monitoring equipment, the training of nurses in electronic monitoring (rather than intermittent auscultation), and the integration of monitoring into hospital workflows create path dependencies that resist change even when evidence supports alternatives.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Medical device and pharmaceutical companies profit from the volume of interventions in US birth care. Their marketing targets hospitals and providers with equipment, supplies, and training designed for the high-intervention model. Fetal monitoring companies sell equipment that generates recurring revenue (maintenance, upgrades, disposable sensors) and whose use is reinforced by malpractice fears ('if you don't monitor continuously and something goes wrong, you'll be sued'). The companies have no financial incentive to promote evidence-based alternatives (intermittent auscultation, physiologic birth management) that would reduce equipment utilization. The result is a self-reinforcing system: equipment is purchased, nurses are trained to use it, protocols are written around it, and malpractice standards are defined by it — making the evidence-based alternative seem like the deviation from standard care, even though the evidence supports the alternative.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Netherlands — Midwife-Led Care as Default System
reform_in_other_systems.description: The Netherlands structures maternity care around a principle called 'primary care at the center': midwives are the primary providers for all pregnancies presumed to be low-risk. Women are 'risked into' hospital-based obstetric care only if specific medical indications arise (defined by the Obstetric Indication List — a nationally agreed-upon clinical criteria set). Approximately 50% of births are managed by midwives in primary care. Home birth remains common (~13% of births) and is fully integrated into the healthcare system with rapid hospital transfer protocols (average transfer time: 15-20 minutes). The maternity care system includes: free universal prenatal care (covered by basic health insurance); 'kraamzorg' — postpartum home care provided by maternity care assistants for 8-10 days after birth, approximately 3 hours per day, providing newborn care, breastfeeding support, household help, and clinical monitoring of maternal recovery; and universal postpartum follow-up. The system produces a maternal mortality rate of approximately 3.4 per 100,000 — roughly one-seventh the US rate — at approximately half the per-birth cost.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrates that making midwifery the default — rather than the exception requiring special advocacy, legal battles, and physician permission — produces dramatically better outcomes at lower cost. The Dutch model inverts the US approach: instead of requiring midwives to justify their right to practice through physician collaboration agreements and regulatory barriers, the system requires medical justification for departing from midwife-led care. The burden of proof is on the system to demonstrate why a healthy woman needs a surgeon, not on the midwife to demonstrate why she can provide care. The result: lower C-section rates (~17% vs. 32% in the US), lower intervention rates, higher satisfaction, vastly lower maternal mortality, and lower cost. The kraamzorg postpartum care system — providing skilled home support for the first 8-10 days — directly addresses the postpartum complications that kill American women: hemorrhage, infection, hypertension, and depression are detected and managed at home before they become emergencies.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Finland — Maternity Box and Universal Prenatal System (Neuvola)
reform_in_other_systems.description: Finland's neuvola ('advice clinic') system provides universal, free maternity and child health clinic services throughout pregnancy, birth, and early childhood — the same provider or small team caring for a family from the first prenatal visit through the child's school entry. Every expectant parent receives a maternity box ('äitiyspakkaus') containing approximately 50 items of essential baby supplies (clothing, bedding, care products, a small mattress — the box itself serves as the baby's first bed) or a cash grant of €170. Eligibility for the box requires engagement with prenatal care by the end of the first trimester — creating a universal incentive for early prenatal care entry. The neuvola system includes: 10-15 prenatal visits (versus 8-12 recommended in the US, with actual utilization often lower); routine screening for domestic violence, substance use, and depression; dental care (integrated into prenatal care); postpartum home visits by the same provider; and child health monitoring through age 7. Finland's maternal mortality rate is approximately 3 per 100,000 — one-seventh the US rate. Infant mortality is approximately 2.1 per 1,000 versus 5.4 in the US.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The neuvola model demonstrates several principles the US system violates: (1) continuity of care — the same provider throughout pregnancy and postpartum — produces trust, earlier symptom detection, and better outcomes versus the US model where a birthing person may see a dozen different providers; (2) universal access (no income test, no copay, no network restriction) eliminates the coverage gaps that kill American women; (3) integration of psychosocial screening into routine prenatal care addresses depression, domestic violence, and substance use before they become crises; (4) the maternity box, while symbolically important, functions primarily as a prenatal care engagement tool — tying a material benefit to an early prenatal visit, achieving near-universal first-trimester entry into care. Finland built this system after WWII when the country was poor, war-devastated, and largely rural — demonstrating that the investment in maternal-child health was a deliberate nation-building priority, not a luxury affordable only to wealthy peacetime societies. Finland's infant mortality dropped from among the highest in Europe (65 per 1,000 in 1938) to among the lowest (2.1 per 1,000) within two generations.

reform_in_other_systems.name: United Kingdom — NHS Midwifery-Led Continuity of Care (Better Births)
reform_in_other_systems.description: The UK's National Maternity Review ('Better Births,' 2016) recommended that all women should have access to midwifery-led continuity of care — the same midwife (or small team of 4-6 midwives) providing care throughout pregnancy, birth, and the postpartum period. Midwives are the lead professional for approximately 50% of births in England. The NHS also provides: free universal prenatal care (no copay, no deductible, no network restriction); home birth support with NHS midwife attendance (approximately 2% of births, with systematic clinical protocols for transfer); alongside midwifery units (AMUs) within hospitals for midwife-led birth with immediate obstetric backup; freestanding midwifery units for low-risk births; and universal postpartum home visits (midwife visits at days 1, 3, 5, and 10 postpartum at minimum, with additional visits based on need). UK maternal mortality is approximately 5.5 per 100,000 — one-quarter the US rate. The NHS spends approximately £3,000 per birth versus $15,000-$30,000+ in the US.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: The Cochrane Review on midwifery-led continuity of care models (Sandall et al., 2016 — analyzing 15 randomized controlled trials involving 17,674 women) found that continuity models compared to standard fragmented care produced: 24% reduction in preterm birth (RR 0.76); 16% reduction in fetal/neonatal death (RR 0.84); 19% reduction in instrumental vaginal birth; 16% reduction in episiotomy; higher satisfaction with care; and no difference in cesarean section rates. The UK model demonstrates that relationship-based midwifery care — knowing the name and face of the person who will be with you when you give birth — is not a luxury but a measurable clinical intervention with outcomes superior to the US fragmented model where a birthing person may meet their delivering provider for the first time during labor. The UK system's challenge is implementation: achieving midwifery continuity at scale requires adequate midwife staffing (the UK faces a midwife shortage of approximately 2,500 FTE), and progress toward the Better Births targets has been slower than planned.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Brazil — Community Health Workers and Programa Saúde da Família
reform_in_other_systems.description: Brazil's Family Health Strategy (Estratégia Saúde da Família, ESF) deploys approximately 265,000 community health agents (agentes comunitários de saúde, ACS) to conduct door-to-door visits in underserved communities — including prenatal check-ins, postpartum follow-up, newborn assessment, immunization tracking, and health education. Each ACS covers approximately 150 families (750 individuals) and visits each family monthly. The ACS program is embedded within Family Health Teams (physician, nurse, nurse technician, and 4-6 ACS per team), each covering approximately 1,000 families. The program reaches approximately 150 million Brazilians — over 70% of the population. Brazil reduced maternal mortality by 56% between 1990 and 2015 (from approximately 120 to 44 per 100,000) — one of the largest absolute reductions achieved by any country during the Millennium Development Goals period. The ESF model was specifically designed to reach populations that facility-based care cannot: rural communities, urban favelas, Indigenous territories, and other settings where distance, cost, discrimination, and distrust prevent healthcare utilization.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrates that community health workers — not physicians, not even midwives, but trained community members earning modest wages (~$400/month in Brazil) — can dramatically reduce maternal mortality in underserved populations when they are systematically deployed, supervised, and integrated into the healthcare system. The ACS model works by: bringing care to people rather than waiting for people to come to facilities; identifying high-risk pregnancies through universal home-based screening; ensuring prenatal care attendance through personal follow-up; detecting postpartum warning signs through home visits; and building trust with communities that have historically been excluded from or harmed by formal healthcare systems. The model is directly relevant to US maternity care deserts: in over 1,100 US counties with no obstetric provider, community health workers could provide the prenatal monitoring, postpartum follow-up, and emergency identification that no physician or facility can provide because none exists. Several US states (Oregon, New Mexico, Minnesota) are piloting CHW-based maternal health programs modeled partly on the Brazilian approach.

reform_in_other_systems.name: New Zealand — Midwifery Partnership Model and Lead Maternity Carer (LMC) System
reform_in_other_systems.description: New Zealand's maternity system allows women to choose a Lead Maternity Carer (LMC) — a midwife, GP, or obstetrician — who provides continuity of care throughout pregnancy, birth, and 6 weeks postpartum. Approximately 94% of women choose a midwife as their LMC. The LMC midwife is self-employed, contracted by the Ministry of Health, and provides services free of charge to the woman. The midwife-woman relationship is structured as a 'partnership' — a formally defined relationship model (the Midwifery Partnership Model developed by Guilliland and Pairman, 1995) that emphasizes shared decision-making, informed consent, and cultural responsiveness. The system explicitly acknowledges the Treaty of Waitangi and the need for culturally appropriate care for Māori women. Maternity care is free for all New Zealand residents regardless of income or immigration status. New Zealand's maternal mortality rate is approximately 7 per 100,000 — roughly one-third the US rate.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrates the effectiveness of a system where: midwifery is the autonomous default (not requiring physician supervision); continuity of care is the standard (one midwife throughout, not a rotating cast of providers); the care relationship is structured as a partnership rather than a hierarchy; cultural responsiveness is embedded in the model (Māori-led maternity services, bicultural training for all midwives); and maternity care is universally free. New Zealand's system was transformed in the early 1990s when the Nurses Amendment Act (1990) granted midwives autonomous practice and direct funding — removing the physician gatekeeping that had limited midwifery practice. Within a decade, midwifery-led care became the norm. The New Zealand model is particularly relevant to the US because: it demonstrates that legislative change (removing physician supervision requirements) rapidly transforms care delivery; it operates within a mixed public-private healthcare system; and its cultural partnership model offers a framework for addressing racial disparities in maternal care.

issues.name: Mental health system collapse
issues.description: Deinstitutionalization closed state psychiatric hospitals with the promise of community-based care that was never funded. The result is a mental health system that effectively does not exist for the severely mentally ill, who cycle through emergency rooms, jails, and homelessness. The largest mental health facilities in America are now jails.

reformers.name: Dr. Thomas Insel
reformers.description: Former NIMH Director (2002-2015) who has become one of the most prominent critics of the mental health system he once led. Author of 'Healing: Our Path from Mental Illness to Mental Health' (2022), which argues that despite $250 billion in annual mental health spending and 30 years of neuroscience advances, outcomes (suicide rates, incarceration rates, homelessness) have gotten worse, not better. His central argument: the system is optimized for diagnosis and medication, not for recovery — and recovery requires housing, employment, community, and purpose, not just pills.

reformers.name: DJ Jaffe and the Treatment Advocacy Center
reformers.description: The Treatment Advocacy Center is the leading organization advocating for reforms to involuntary treatment laws, expansion of Assisted Outpatient Treatment (AOT/Kendra's Law), and increased psychiatric bed capacity. Their reports have documented that the US has fewer psychiatric beds per capita (11.7 per 100,000) than it had in 1850, and that jails and prisons now house 10 times more seriously mentally ill individuals than state hospitals. The organization occupies a controversial but empirically grounded position: that some individuals with severe mental illness lack insight into their condition and that compassionate coercion (court-ordered outpatient treatment) saves lives.

reformers.name: National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI)
reformers.description: Largest grassroots mental health advocacy organization in the US, with over 600 local affiliates. Provides peer support, family education, crisis intervention, and policy advocacy. NAMI was instrumental in passing the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. Their annual survey data documents the treatment gap: only 47% of adults with mental illness received treatment in 2022, and the average delay between symptom onset and treatment is 11 years.

reformers.name: Dr. Christine Yu Moutier and the American Foundation for Suicide Prevention
reformers.description: AFSP is the largest private funder of suicide prevention research in the US. Dr. Moutier, as Chief Medical Officer, has championed the public health approach to suicide prevention: means restriction (gun storage, bridge barriers), crisis intervention (988 lifeline), upstream prevention (school-based programs), and reduction of provider burnout. AFSP's advocacy was central to establishing the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (2022), which received over 5 million contacts in its first year.

reformers.name: Judge Steve Leifman
reformers.description: Miami-Dade County judge who pioneered the Criminal Mental Health Project — diverting individuals with serious mental illness from the criminal justice system into treatment. The program has reduced recidivism by 75% among participants and saved Miami-Dade an estimated $12 million annually in jail costs. Leifman's model has been replicated in over 20 jurisdictions and demonstrates that treatment-based diversion is both more humane and more cost-effective than incarceration.

legislation.name: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
legislation.description: Established a nationwide three-digit crisis number (replacing the 10-digit National Suicide Prevention Lifeline) in July 2022. Received over 5 million calls, texts, and chats in its first year. Funded through the National Suicide Hotline Designation Act of 2020. However, call volume has exposed capacity gaps: average wait times vary significantly by state, some calls are abandoned before connection, and follow-up services after crisis contact remain inadequate.

legislation.name: Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)
legislation.description: Requires group health plans that cover mental health to provide benefits no more restrictive than medical/surgical benefits — parity in financial requirements (copays, deductibles) and treatment limitations (visit limits, prior authorization). Passed in 2008, strengthened by ACA in 2010, and further tightened by 2024 final rules requiring comparative analyses of non-quantitative treatment limitations. Despite the law, enforcement has been weak: insurers routinely impose prior authorization, network inadequacy, and reimbursement rates for mental health that are 20-40% below medical rates — violating the spirit if not the letter of parity.

legislation.name: IMD Exclusion (Institutions for Mental Disease — Medicaid prohibition)
legislation.description: Federal Medicaid law prohibits payment for care in psychiatric facilities with more than 16 beds for adults aged 21-64. Enacted in 1965 to prevent warehousing, the exclusion has become a structural barrier to psychiatric care: it means Medicaid — the largest payer for mental health services — cannot pay for inpatient psychiatric treatment in most settings. The exclusion drove deinstitutionalization (states closed hospitals to avoid paying without federal matching funds) and continues to prevent the development of adequate psychiatric bed capacity.

legislation.name: Deinstitutionalization without Community Reinvestment (structural gap)
legislation.description: Between 1955 and 2016, the US reduced state psychiatric hospital beds from 559,000 to approximately 38,000 — a 93% reduction. The Community Mental Health Act (1963) promised community-based care to replace institutional care. The community care was never funded at the promised scale. The result: individuals with serious mental illness were discharged from hospitals into communities without housing, treatment, or support — and many ended up homeless, incarcerated, or dead. This is not a single law but a structural failure spanning 60 years of bipartisan neglect.

legislation.name: Certified Community Behavioral Health Clinics (CCBHCs)
legislation.description: A promising model: federally certified clinics required to provide comprehensive mental health and substance use services to anyone regardless of ability to pay, with enhanced Medicaid reimbursement (cost-based rather than fee-for-service). Started as a demonstration program in 8 states in 2017, expanded to over 500 clinics in 46 states by 2024. CCBHCs have shown 60% reductions in psychiatric ED visits and 40% reductions in jail bookings among their populations. Bipartisan legislation to make the model permanent and nationwide has been introduced.

structurally_incentivized.name: Private Insurance Industry (Parity Evasion)
structurally_incentivized.description: Despite the Mental Health Parity Act, private insurers systematically underpay for mental health services. Reimbursement rates for psychiatrists are 20-40% below rates for comparable medical specialists. Network adequacy for mental health is routinely worse than for medical care — patients are 5x more likely to use out-of-network providers for mental health than for medical care, indicating that in-network supply is artificially constrained.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Insurers manage mental health costs by making access difficult: low reimbursement rates drive providers out of network, prior authorization creates administrative barriers, narrow networks create geographic access gaps, and the complexity of filing claims deters both providers and patients. The result is functional non-coverage — the plan 'covers' mental health but the covered services are inaccessible. Enforcement of parity violations is slow, complaint-driven, and under-resourced.

structurally_incentivized.name: Criminal Justice System (Jails as Default Psychiatric Facilities)
structurally_incentivized.description: The three largest mental health facilities in America are the Cook County Jail (Chicago), LA County Jail, and Rikers Island — none of which are mental health facilities. An estimated 44% of jail inmates and 37% of prison inmates have a diagnosed mental health condition. The criminal justice system has become the de facto mental health system because it cannot refuse intake — unlike hospitals, which can divert, or insurers, which can deny.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Jails receive per-diem funding for inmates regardless of mental health status. Mental health diversion reduces jail population and therefore jail funding. Sheriffs' departments, correctional officers' unions, and the private prison industry (CoreCivic, GEO Group) have financial interests in maintaining the incarcerated population. The system is not designed to treat mental illness, but it is funded to contain it.

structurally_incentivized.name: Pharmaceutical Industry (Medication-Centric Treatment Model)
structurally_incentivized.description: The pharmaceutical industry has shaped mental health treatment toward medication-first approaches. Antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics represent a $30+ billion annual US market. Pharmaceutical marketing to psychiatrists, direct-to-consumer advertising, and industry-funded research prioritize pharmacological solutions over psychosocial interventions (therapy, supported housing, employment programs) that have equal or superior long-term evidence.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Drug company revenue is maximized by the medication-centric model: every patient on chronic medication is recurring revenue. Psychosocial interventions (housing, therapy, peer support, employment) generate no pharmaceutical revenue. Industry funding of psychiatric research, continuing medical education, and clinical guidelines systematically elevates medication evidence and marginalizes evidence for non-pharmacological approaches. The result: 80% of psychiatric visits result in a prescription, while only 33% include psychotherapy.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Trieste Model — Community-Based Mental Health (Italy)
reform_in_other_systems.description: Trieste, Italy, closed its psychiatric hospitals in the 1970s under the leadership of Franco Basaglia and replaced them with a network of 24-hour community mental health centers, supported housing, social cooperatives (employment), and crisis respite services. No one with mental illness is turned away. The system operates on the principle that recovery happens in the community, not in institutions.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: WHO designated Trieste a global model for community mental health. The city has among the lowest involuntary commitment rates and psychiatric bed utilization in Europe while maintaining safety and community integration. The Trieste model demonstrates that closing institutions CAN work — if the community alternatives are actually built and funded. The US did the first half (closing hospitals) without the second half (building community systems).

reform_in_other_systems.name: Finland — Open Dialogue Approach
