Even if only 5-10% of patients abandon treatment after a denial or delay (and studies suggest the figure is higher), the savings on those abandoned claims substantially exceed the administrative cost of running the prior auth system. The system is designed to create friction: the more difficult it is to obtain authorization, the more patients and providers give up. The insurer captures the savings from abandonment while reporting the denial as a 'clinical review decision.'

structurally_incentivized.name: Prior Authorization Vendors and Utilization Management Companies (eviCore, AIM Specialty Health, Carelon)
structurally_incentivized.description: Third-party utilization management companies process prior auth requests on behalf of insurers. eviCore (owned by Cigna subsidiary Evernorth) processes over 60 million clinical reviews annually. These companies are compensated based on the volume of reviews processed and, in some arrangements, the cost savings they achieve — creating direct financial incentives for denial.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Utilization management companies profit from the existence and expansion of prior auth requirements. Every new service category subject to prior auth generates review volume. Reducing prior auth requirements reduces their business. The companies employ physicians and nurses as reviewers, but the review criteria and algorithmic tools are designed to identify reasons to deny rather than reasons to approve. A reviewer who approves too many requests may face performance management; a reviewer who denies at higher rates generates more 'savings' for the client insurer.

structurally_incentivized.name: Pharmacy Benefit Managers (Express Scripts, CVS Caremark, OptumRx)
structurally_incentivized.description: PBMs impose prior authorization and step therapy requirements on prescription medications, requiring patients to try (and fail on) cheaper medications before accessing the prescribed drug. The three largest PBMs control approximately 80% of the prescription drug market. Prior auth on medications delays treatment and creates administrative burden for both prescribers and patients.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: PBMs earn rebates from pharmaceutical manufacturers that are tied to formulary placement. Prior auth and step therapy requirements steer patients toward drugs that generate higher rebates for the PBM, not necessarily the drugs that are most clinically appropriate. The prior auth system is the enforcement mechanism for rebate-driven formularies: the PBM requires prior auth for the drug the physician prescribed, steering the patient toward the drug that maximizes the PBM's rebate revenue.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Germany — No Prior Authorization for Physician-Ordered Services
reform_in_other_systems.description: Germany's statutory health insurance system does not require prior authorization for physician-ordered services. The physician makes the clinical decision; the sickness fund pays. Cost control is achieved through negotiated fee schedules, prescribing budgets, and peer review — not through pre-service authorization barriers. If a physician orders an MRI, the patient gets an MRI. The idea that an insurance company clerk would overrule a physician's clinical judgment is foreign to the German system.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Germany achieves lower per-capita healthcare spending than the US ($7,400 vs. $12,555) without using prior authorization as a cost-control mechanism. This demonstrates that prior auth is not necessary for cost control — it is a cost-control mechanism chosen by the US insurance industry because it generates profit. Germany controls costs through price regulation and budgetary discipline, not through bureaucratic rationing of individual clinical decisions.

reform_in_other_systems.name: France — Physician Autonomy with Post-Hoc Review
reform_in_other_systems.description: France's system allows physician clinical autonomy for most services. For the 35 designated serious chronic conditions (Affections de Longue Durée), treatment is covered at 100% without any prior authorization. For other services, physicians prescribe and the statutory insurance pays, with post-hoc review for outlier patterns rather than pre-service gatekeeping.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: France achieves WHO's #1-ranked health system performance with physician autonomy and without prior authorization for most services. French patients report virtually no experience of treatment delay due to insurance review. The contrast with the US — where 94% of physicians report care delays from prior auth — illustrates that the prior auth burden is a feature of the US insurance model, not an inherent requirement of healthcare cost management.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Taiwan — Real-Time Claims with Algorithmic Post-Hoc Audit
reform_in_other_systems.description: Taiwan's single-payer NHI processes claims in real-time using smart card technology. There is no prior authorization. Physicians order services; the NHI pays. Cost control is achieved through: the national fee schedule (prices are set), global budgets (total spending is capped), and algorithmic post-hoc audit (AI flags outlier prescribing patterns for peer review after the fact). The patient never experiences a delay due to insurance review.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Taiwan processes approximately 1 million claims per day with average adjudication time under 24 hours. The system costs approximately $1,400 per capita (vs. $12,555 in the US) with higher patient satisfaction and comparable outcomes. Taiwan demonstrates that technology can be used to reduce administrative burden (real-time processing, algorithmic audit) rather than increase it (prior authorization portals, fax-based review). The US uses technology to build more elaborate bureaucratic barriers; Taiwan uses it to eliminate them.
